--- SOURCE 1: Institutional Investors Demand Greater Transparency on ESG ---
URL: https://rethinking65.com/institutional-investors-demand-greater-transparency-on-esg/

SUMMARY:
Institutional investors are demanding that asset managers provide greater transparency in their reporting on ESG investments.

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://www.facebook.com/tr?id=241574441992622&ev=PageView&noscript=1)
![Logo](https://rethinking65.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/r65-logo-rev-2024a.png)
![Logo](https://rethinking65.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/rethinking65-logo-white-new-600px.png)
![Logo](https://rethinking65.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/04/rethinking65-logo-2024.png)

# Institutional Investors Demand Greater Transparency on ESG

Asset managers are being required to provide clear and comprehensive reporting on responsible investing.

![Institutional Investors Demand Greater Transparency](https://rethinking65.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/institutional-investors-696x544-1.jpg "institutional-investors-696x544")

Institutional investors are demanding that asset managers provide greater transparency in their reporting on ESG investments, according to Cerulli Associates, which calls the trend “one of the most significant changes in responsible investing in recent years.”

Stakeholders, who also include regulators, clients and the public, want clear and comprehensive reports on environmental, social and governance-related activities, the latest Cerulli Edge — U.S. Institutional Edition reports.

Cerulli states that 58% of institutional investors require or plan to require asset managers to give them portfolio-level exposure to financially material [ESG](https://rethinking65.com/court-rules-for-advisors-providing-esg-advice/) risks, in addition to impact and thematic reporting. Currently, 23% of asset owners require their asset managers to report on ESG-related engagement activities, and 22% say they will require it in the next two years.

“This demand for transparency reflects the broader push for responsible and accountable business practices across industries,” says Cerulli analyst Gloria Pais in a news release. “Institutional investors want to ensure ESG considerations are not just a passing trend, but a fundamental part of the investment process. As such, asset managers must step up their reporting efforts to meet the expectations of asset owners and remain competitive in the evolving market,” she adds.

## Challenges to ESG Reporting

Cerulli says meeting the new requirements may be a challenge, largely because there are no standardized reporting guidelines for ESG metrics. Asset managers may have difficulty defining ESG risk and impact consistently across different sectors and industries. That makes it difficult for asset owners to compare performance across their portfolios and make informed decisions, Cerulli says.

### Related Posts:

![Untitled design (22)](https://rethinking65.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Untitled-design-22-150x150.png "The 2025 Ex-US Investment Opportunity Set")
![David Leo, coach to financial advisors](https://rethinking65.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/06/WORK-klein-QA-leo-older-coach-art.jpg "At 83, This Coach to Financial Advisors Shares Perspective, Wisdom and Motivation")
![Cancer treatment](https://rethinking65.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/HEALTH-NYT-_medical_startup_death-art1-150x150.jpg "A Startup Claimed Its Device Could Cure Cancer. Then Patients Began Dying.")
![WORK-sullivan-entrepreneur-part1-art](https://rethinking65.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/04/WORK-sullivan-entrepreneur-part1-art.jpg "When Retirees Succeed at New Businesses — And When They Don’t")

Powered by [Contextual Related Posts](https://webberzone.com/plugins/contextual-related-posts/)

The industry is working to standardize ESG reporting terminology and measurement frameworks but faces hurdles. According to the report, 38% of asset owners say defining ESG boundaries is a key challenge, especially when differentiating between ESG and impact investing.

Asset managers involved with [responsible investing](https://rethinking65.com/investment-value-of-nature/) programs need to invest in robust reporting systems that meet these expectations, Cerulli says.

“Comprehensive, standardized reports on ESG risks and impacts can help asset managers demonstrate their commitment to responsible investing and attract more institutional clients,” Pais says. “Furthermore, asset managers at the crossroads of evolving market demands should focus on integrating ESG considerations into their investment processes. These steps will help asset managers remain competitive and align with the values of their institutional clients,” she concludes.

## Trends Pro and Con

Cerulli reports that responsible investing faces increasing political headwinds, causing some institutional investors to drop such investments. The pace of new responsible investing has slowed, a record number of recently listed fund products have closed, and among funds that use or have used the ESG label, 10% have dropped it, according to the report. Among asset owners, 10% have scaled back on incorporating ESG factors into their investment strategies, mostly in response to political pushback.

However, the trend toward responsible investing and diversity in investment management is still gaining momentum, the authors state. Over the past five years, 94% of institutions that invest in responsible investing products have maintained or increased those allocations. Most institutions plan to increase or maintain such investments. However, some plan to reduce allocations to screening (23%) and ESG-focused products (22%).

## Exclusions and Inclusions

Negative exclusionary screening — avoiding investment in companies associated with products considered undesirable — is used by 45% of institutions. Top screens include:

Areas of responsible investing favored by institutions include

## ESG Backlash

Shifting political winds are playing a significant role in the responsible investing landscape. About 10% of asset owners have scaled back ESG considerations in their investment decisions. According Cerulli this is mainly due to political pushback, as more Republican-leaning legislatures have passed laws restricting the use of ESG factors in investment decisions.

Additionally, 34% of institutions that have reduced ESG investing say the primary deterrent was the time-consuming and costly process of responding to backlash. Also among those that have scaled back, 28% say they lost  confidence in the merits of ESG, 24% cite litigation fears, and 14% report external pressure from stakeholders.

## External Advisors Gain Prominence

More asset owners are using external advisors such as investment consultants and outsourced chief investment offices (OCIOs) to address the complexities of ESG reporting and investment management, Cerulli reports.

Such personnel are instrumental in helping asset owners respond to the ongoing ESG backlash, communicating how responsible investing can mitigate risks such as climate change, reputational damage, and regulatory turmoil, according to the report.

# How North American Investors Are Shaping the Future of ESG

![ESG in investment](data:image/png;base64,)
![ESG in investment](https://cse-net.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Blogs-How-North-American-Investors-Are-Shaping-the-Future-of-ESG.jpg)
![Social Media Icon](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)
![Social Media Icon](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)
![Social Media Icon](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**ESG in North American investors** is driving significant changes across industries. Investors are now [prioritizing environmental, social, and governance (ESG) factors](https://www.esgtoday.com/80-of-global-investors-now-have-sustainable-investment-policies-in-place-deloitte-tufts-survey/) alongside financial performance, reshaping how businesses operate and report on sustainability.

#### **Investor Demand for ESG Disclosures**

Investor demand for transparency is at an all-time high, with **ESG in North American investors** at the forefront[. Institutional investors](https://www.esginvestor.net/institutional-investors-progress-on-climate-action-plans/) expect detailed ESG disclosures to ensure companies are committed to sustainable practices. Failing to meet these expectations risks losing key investment opportunities.

Investors are focused on how businesses manage environmental risks, employee treatment, and governance transparency. As a result, many North American companies are enhancing their [ESG reporting](https://www.sustainableviews.com/in-charts-regulation-is-pushing-companies-to-enhance-their-esg-reporting-9c933255/) to stay competitive. In fact:

#### **ESG’s Influence on Investment Decisions**

ESG has become a [key factor in investment decisions](https://cse-net.org/which-esg-related-criteria-influence-investment-decisions/). Companies with strong ESG performance are seen as lower risk and more resilient, attracting capital more easily. Investors also avoid companies with potential future liabilities tied to environmental or governance issues, safeguarding their portfolios.

#### **Case Studies: Companies Attracting ESG-Conscious Investors**

Several companies are leading the way in attracting **ESG-focused investors**:

As **ESG in North American investors** continues to grow, companies aligning with sustainability and transparency will secure long-term success. ESG is becoming a critical factor in both financial and strategic decision-making.

#### **About the [Center for Sustainability](https://cse-net.org/2025-sustainability-esg-research-findings/) and Excellence (CSE)**

[The Center for Sustainability and Excellence (CSE)](https://cse-net.org/) has been at the forefront of industry trends for nearly 20 years, providing top-quality certified training in Sustainability (ESG) for C-suite executives and Corporate Responsibility managers worldwide.

With North American investors emphasizing sustainability, transparency, and ethical governance, learning how to align your organization with these trends will be crucial to your organization’s financial success. Consider joining our **Certified Sustainability (ESG) Practitioner Program, Advanced Edition 2024** to deepen your insights into how to integrate ESG into your operations, making your business more attractive to investors—sign up [here](https://cse-net.org/trainings/usa-sustainability-esg-course-cohort4/).

--- SOURCE 3: Individual Investors Demand ESG Data They Can Trust - Newsroom ---
URL: https://newsroom.workiva.com/press-releases/power-transparency-individual-investors-demand-esg-data-they-can-trust

SUMMARY:
The survey revealed transparency around ESG disclosures is becoming integral to investor decisions, and companies will undoubtedly experience increased pressure

FULL CONTENT:
## Main navigation

# The Power of Transparency: Individual Investors Demand ESG Data They Can Trust

***New Workiva Survey Identifies Top Trends for 2021:***

AMES, Iowa--(BUSINESS WIRE)-- [Workiva Inc.](https://cts.businesswire.com/ct/CT?id=smartlink&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.workiva.com%2Fabout&esheet=52434814&newsitemid=20210525005313&lan=en-US&anchor=Workiva+Inc.&index=1&md5=01f44a747c9088f950dbcb7d509dbcb5) (NYSE:WK) today released findings from a survey of individual investors that reveal approximately 70% of respondents believe organizations have a responsibility to demonstrate ESG performance to investors, with Gen Z and younger Millennial-aged investors (18-34 year-olds) the most demanding around ESG credentials. The survey revealed transparency around ESG disclosures is becoming integral to investor decisions, and companies will undoubtedly experience increased pressure from investors to be able to report on their progress.

“Our survey findings represent a powerful motivation for organizations to take a serious look at how they are reporting ESG and other non-financial data,” said Julie Iskow, chief operating officer of Workiva. “We believe there is a real competitive advantage in attracting today’s modern investors with a commitment to corporate transparency.”

**Top Survey Trends:**

***Younger Investors Know What They Want******.***Awareness around ESG is especially high within the younger generation, with 84% being aware of ESG, and of this group nearly two-thirds (64%) claiming to know a lot about it.

***Difficulty in Trusting Data.***When asked if they found it difficult to trust what companies are disclosing about what they’re doing for the environment and society, only 15% of respondents disagreed.

***Data is the Key.***Over one-third of survey respondents (43%) in all markets noted they trust ESG performance more when reported through numbers and data than qualitative descriptions — this increased to 54% for 18-34 year-olds.

This survey of individual investors comes on the heels of large institutional investors and asset managers extolling the benefits of integrating ESG metrics as a key factor in building their investment portfolios, as well as trends in new regulations requiring non-financial reporting.

“The survey indicates that investors want to see earnings growth, deeper data transparency and progress in all areas of ESG. This will require a technology solution that can deliver accuracy and simplify the complex process of collecting and reporting non-financial data; Workiva is revolutionizing how companies approach ESG reporting with a solution that does just that,” added Iskow.

Workiva's cloud-based platform creates a connected and controlled ecosystem that streamlines the integration of financial and non-financial information. ESG and finance teams can collect, assemble and report data on one secure platform, allowing businesses to keep pace with the rapidly growing demand for trusted, transparent data and proof of ESG forward-looking business goals.

Check out the [ESG Survey results](https://cts.businesswire.com/ct/CT?id=smartlink&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.workiva.com%2Fnewsroom%2Fworkiva-fact-sheet-individual-investors-esg-attitudes-data&esheet=52434814&newsitemid=20210525005313&lan=en-US&anchor=ESG+Survey+results&index=2&md5=bebc6bda13e28c1546b92c86c9f1b05a) or learn more about Workiva’s purpose-built [ESG reporting solution](https://cts.businesswire.com/ct/CT?id=smartlink&url=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.workiva.com%2Fsolutions%2Fesg-reporting&esheet=52434814&newsitemid=20210525005313&lan=en-US&anchor=ESG+reporting+solution&index=3&md5=38c55f120a4504dc8c2fce7963316500).

**About the Survey**

Workiva commissioned a survey to better understand the current sentiment of individual investors (who have grown in importance and activity1) on the transparency and faith in ESG performance and reporting data. The survey findings cover individual investors from the United States, United Kingdom, Germany and France. Read our [Fact Sheet](https://cts.businesswire.com/ct/CT?id=smartlink&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.workiva.com%2Fnewsroom%2Fworkiva-fact-sheet-individual-investors-esg-attitudes-data&esheet=52434814&newsitemid=20210525005313&lan=en-US&anchor=Fact+Sheet&index=4&md5=023f13b83383ae7a0f41f1a1b5caf2a6) for methodology and specific question responses.

1. Individual investors made up an estimated 19.5% of U.S. equity trading volume according to *Bloomberg Intelligence,* a jump of over 4% compared to 2019.

**About Workiva**

Workiva Inc. (NYSE: WK) simplifies complex work for thousands of organizations worldwide. Customers trust Workiva’s open, intelligent and intuitive platform to connect data, documents and teams. The results: improved efficiency, greater transparency and less risk. Learn more at workiva.com.

--- SOURCE 5: Asset Owners Increasingly Demand Transparency… ---
URL: https://www.cerulli.com/press-releases/asset-owners-increasingly-demand-transparency-around-responsible-investing

SUMMARY:
Asset owners now require asset managers to provide clear and comprehensive reports on environmental, social, and governance (ESG)-related activities.

FULL CONTENT:
# Cerulli Associates

# Asset Owners Increasingly Demand Transparency Around Responsible Investing

February 19, 2025 — Boston

Institutional investors seek clear and comprehensive reporting from asset managers

One of the most significant changes in responsible investing in recent years is the growing demand for transparency in reporting, according to the latest [***Cerulli Edge—U.S. Institutional Edition***](https://www.cerulli.com/edge/us-institutional-edge). With increasing pressure from stakeholders, including regulators, clients, and the public, asset owners now require asset managers to provide clear and comprehensive reports on environmental, social, and governance (ESG)-related activities.

Cerulli’s research finds more than half of institutional investors (58%) require or plan to require asset managers to provide portfolio-level exposure to financially material ESG risks, along with impact and thematic reporting. Furthermore, 23% of asset owners require their asset managers to report on ESG-related engagement activities—a similar proportion (22%) will implement this requirement within the next two years.

“This demand for transparency reflects the broader push for responsible and accountable business practices across industries,” says Gloria Pais, analyst. “Institutional investors want to ensure ESG considerations are not just a passing trend, but a fundamental part of the investment process. As such, asset managers must step up their reporting efforts to meet the expectations of asset owners and remain competitive in the evolving market,” she adds.

Achieving this level of transparency is not without its challenges. One of the biggest obstacles is the lack of standardized reporting guidelines for ESG metrics. Many asset owners struggle to define ESG risk and impact consistently across different sectors and industries. This lack of consistency makes it difficult for asset owners to compare performance across their portfolios and make informed decisions.

The industry's efforts to standardize terminology and measurement frameworks for ESG reporting are ongoing, but significant hurdles remain. More than one-third of asset owners (38%) cite the difficulty of defining ESG boundaries as a key challenge, particularly when differentiating between ESG and impact investing. Asset owners face the daunting task of aligning their investment decisions with the broader ESG framework without consistent reporting standards.

As asset owners demand greater transparency and ESG accountability, those asset managers committed to helping their clients navigate responsible investing programs should invest in robust reporting systems that meet these expectations.

“Comprehensive, standardized reports on ESG risks and impacts can help asset managers demonstrate their commitment to responsible investing and attract more institutional clients,” says Pais. “Furthermore, asset managers at the crossroads of evolving market demands should focus on integrating ESG considerations into their investment processes. These steps will help asset managers remain competitive and align with the values of their institutional clients,” she concludes.

DESCRIPTION: The image features the acronym "ESG" filled with a lush green forest pattern, with the words "Environmental Social Governance" written below, emphasizing the importance of transparency and accountability in environmental, social, and governance reporting.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

enriched_points.expanded_content: Institutional investors increasingly require detailed ESG disclosures, emphasizing the need for clear reporting on environmental, social, and governance factors to assess risks and opportunities.
Today's consumers are more conscious of corporate sustainability practices, driving companies to adopt responsible practices and transparently report their ESG commitments to retain customer loyalty.
Activists pressure companies to improve their ESG practices, advocating for accountability and transparency, which can significantly impact corporate policies and public perception.
A strong commitment to ESG principles enhances corporate reputation, attracting investors and customers while mitigating risks associated with potential scandals and negative publicity.

Organizations are aligning their strategies with long-term sustainability goals, integrating ESG factors to drive operational efficiency, innovation, and compliance with evolving regulatory standards.
